storylines.moment: US strikes Abadan refinery — oldest in Middle East; only 14 ships crossed Hormuz Sunday vs 130/day before war
storylines.why_now: US strikes today hit Abadan, Mahshahr, Qeshm Island, and Kish Island. The Strait has effectively shut: 14 ships crossed Sunday vs the normal ~130 per day, representing a fifth of world oil supply. Trump demanded a 20% cargo fee from countries that depend on Hormuz oil and warned he'll strike bridges and power plants 'next week' unless Tehran returns to the table. Iran hit two ships in Omani waters, killing a crew member.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does a strait 21 miles wide at its narrowest determine the price of oil in every country on earth?

history.note: US hits 90 targets across Iran's coastline; Iran retaliates against US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain
Pakistan and Qatar mediate; Trump says agreed to talks but ceasefire still 'over'; Strait of Hormuz remains the sticking point as shipping slows
Trump announces US will reinstate blockade of Strait, charge ships for passage. US Central Command: 140 Iran targets struck Sunday. Oil up 3.6% to $78.7/bbl.
IRGC hits two supertankers in Strait overnight. Trump reverses ship-fee proposal within 24 hours. Naval blockade reimposed; Iran formally declares ceasefire void.

US strikes hit Abadan refinery (oldest in Middle East), Mahshahr, Qeshm, Kish Island. Only 14 ships crossed Hormuz Sunday vs ~130/day before war. Trump demands 20% cargo fee; threatens to strike power plants next week. Iran hits two ships in Omani waters, kills one crew member.

storylines.moment: 30+ countries pledge to defend Ukraine; Zelenskyy meets Macron in Paris on Bastille Day
storylines.why_now: More than 30 countries formally pledged to defend Ukraine against Russia today, announced alongside Zelenskyy's Bastille Day meeting with Macron in Paris. The joint EU ballistic missile defense framework announced this morning is the most concrete European security commitment since the war began. Ukraine's shadow fleet campaign — 36 tankers struck — continues in the background.
storylines.hidden_question: How did Russia build a secret fleet of hundreds of oil tankers that half the world quietly agreed to pretend not to see?

history.note: Russia launches mass attack: 351 drones, 68 missiles kill at least 22 in Kyiv
Ukraine shifts to offense: 36 shadow fleet tankers struck in 4 days; first-ever strike on Omsk refinery, 2,500km inside Russia
Ukraine and European partners announce joint ballistic missile defense framework. Zelenskyy meets Macron in Paris on Bastille Day.
30+ countries formally pledge to defend Ukraine; announcement made alongside Zelenskyy-Macron meeting in Paris.

storylines.moment: Ebola spreads to new regions of Congo — WHO tracking cases beyond Ituri epicenter
storylines.why_now: New suspected Ebola cases have appeared in parts of the Democratic Republic of Congo previously unaffected, signaling the outbreak is moving beyond the Ituri epicenter. WHO is monitoring; no international travel alert yet.
storylines.hidden_question: Why, with a highly effective Ebola vaccine available since 2020, does the same outbreak keep returning to the same region of Congo?

storylines.moment: Spain wildfire kills multiple in Almeria — victims found in burned-out vehicles
storylines.why_now: A fast-moving fire in the southern province of Almeria, a major holiday destination, trapped people in their cars as they tried to flee. Multiple fatalities confirmed. Coming weeks after Europe's early-summer heat surge.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do wildfires keep catching people in their cars even when they had time to evacuate?

storylines.moment: Graham's sister Darline sworn in to his Senate seat today — oath at 2:30pm EST
storylines.why_now: South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster appointed Darline Graham Nordone, Senator Graham's younger sister, to complete his term. Her oath of office was scheduled for 2:30pm EST today. Graham died Saturday of an aortic dissection hours after returning from Kyiv.
storylines.hidden_question: How did one of the most reliable opponents of democratic accountability at home become one of the Senate's most consistent champions of democracy abroad?

history.note: Graham dies at 71 of aortic dissection; flown back from Kyiv the day before
Governor McMaster appoints Graham's younger sister Darline Graham Nordone to his seat; oath scheduled 2:30pm EST today

storylines.moment: Bangkok pub fire kills 27 — no fire escapes, exit doors reportedly locked
storylines.why_now: A fire broke out shortly before midnight Sunday at the Na Ladprao pub in Bangkok after smoke emerged from a circuit breaker near the stage. Most victims were found in windowless bathrooms near a rear exit. Firefighters brought the blaze under control in 30 minutes; 63 others hospitalized. Investigation underway.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do nightclub fires keep killing people the same way — locked exits, no sprinklers, crowds that can't find the way out — in every country, decade after decade?

storylines.moment: Heat dome expands east — Midwest hits today, Northeast tomorrow, 100°F+ possible
storylines.why_now: The heat system that set 148 daily records between June 30 and July 5 is now pushing into the Midwest and East Coast. Billings, MT could top 110°F for the first time in a century. Morning lows in cities staying in the mid-upper 70s — offering little overnight relief. Health alerts in effect across the region.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do we still measure heat waves by the daily high when it's the overnight low that actually kills people?

storylines.moment: DOJ opens investigation into Yale admissions — undergrad, law, and medical school
storylines.why_now: The Justice Department is investigating Yale's admissions practices across all three major divisions. Coming after Harvard and other elite institutions faced similar scrutiny, the investigation focuses on whether Yale's process complies with anti-discrimination law post the Supreme Court's 2023 affirmative action ruling.
storylines.hidden_question: After the Supreme Court banned race-conscious admissions, why do elite universities still end up with roughly the same kinds of students?

storylines.moment: Rubio calls ICC 'unelected globalist bureaucrats'; more officials sanctioned; ICC judges sue US in Manhattan
storylines.why_now: Secretary of State Rubio announced new ICC sanctions today, describing the court as 'a global tribunal staffed by unelected globalist bureaucrats' targeting Americans and Israelis whose countries never joined. ICC judges filed a lawsuit in Manhattan federal court challenging the sanctions. The US is the only major democracy sanctioning an international criminal tribunal.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does the world's largest democracy impose sanctions on the court that was built to prosecute genocide?

history.note: State Dept vows to 'disable' ICC; ICC judges file lawsuit in Manhattan against sanctions
Rubio announces additional ICC official sanctions; describes court as 'unelected globalist bureaucrats targeting Americans and Israelis'

storylines.moment: New York bans new AI data centers for 12 months — first US state; electricity rates up 68% in 6 years
storylines.why_now: Governor Hochul signed an executive order today barring new large-scale data centers (50MW+ of power) for up to one year while the state assesses environmental and grid impacts. New York's residential electricity rates have risen 68% over the past six years — ranking it the 4th most expensive state for power. The ban affects Microsoft, Amazon, and Google projects. 46% of New Yorkers support the moratorium; only 21% oppose.
storylines.hidden_question: Why is the cost of AI now showing up on Americans' electricity bills before it's showing up in their paychecks?

storylines.moment: S&P downgrades Oracle to one notch above junk as AI spending hits $90B capex
storylines.why_now: S&P cut Oracle to BBB- on July 9 — the lowest investment grade — citing $167B in debt, $90–95B projected capex for FY2027, and the fact that OpenAI alone accounts for roughly half its remaining performance obligations. Oracle's stock rose anyway.
storylines.hidden_question: Why did Oracle's stock rise the same day S&P warned it was approaching junk — and what does that say about how investors are betting on AI?

storylines.moment: IBM confirmed $67B market cap wiped — worst day since Black Monday 1987; tech sells off on IBM contagion
storylines.why_now: IBM's crash is now confirmed at 25-26%, erasing $67B in market cap — worse than its October 19, 1987 drop of 23.7% on Black Monday. Q2 revenue was $17.2B vs $17.9B expected; EPS $2.27 vs $3.01. CEO Arvind Krishna blamed clients shifting to servers, storage, and memory in the final weeks of June. Contagion hit ServiceNow, Salesforce, and Accenture. Full earnings July 22.
storylines.hidden_question: What happens to inflation when the peace deal that lowered gas prices collapses?

history.note: Brent reverses to $76+ as conflict enters day two; Iranian retaliation against US bases confirmed
Oil 'relatively tame' as full-scale war doubts grow; SK Hynix +13% debut adds bullish AI-chip counterweight
Brent crude jumps 3.6% to $78.7/bbl on Trump's Strait blockade announcement.
June CPI: 3.5% annual vs 3.9% expected; gas -9.7% in June (ceasefire effect, now gone). IBM crashes 24-25%.
IBM confirmed down 25-26%; $67B in market cap wiped — worse than Black Monday 1987. Q2 revenue $17.2B vs $17.9B expected. Full earnings July 22.

storylines.moment: IBM crashes 25% — worst day in 115-year history, $67B gone — as clients abandon software for AI hardware
storylines.why_now: IBM Q2 missed on revenue ($17.2B vs $17.9B expected) and EPS ($2.27 vs $3.01). CEO Arvind Krishna said clients in the final weeks of June shifted their quarterly capex from IBM software contracts to servers, storage, and memory — securing supply-constrained AI infrastructure ahead of expected price increases. The crash was worse than IBM's Black Monday 1987 drop. Contagion: ServiceNow -7%, Salesforce -5%, Accenture -8%.
storylines.hidden_question: Why did IBM — which helped invent deep learning and supplies the mainframes that run the global financial system — keep ending up on the wrong side of the AI wave it helped start?

history.note: IBM issues preliminary Q2 warning; shares crater 24-25% intraday
IBM closes down 25-26%; $67B in market cap erased. Confirmed worst day in company history, exceeding Black Monday 1987 drop of 23.7%.

storylines.moment: SpaceX joined Nasdaq-100 on July 7 via rewritten rules — $4B+ in forced ETF buying
storylines.why_now: SpaceX (SPCX) joined the Nasdaq-100 effective July 7 — just 15 trading days after its IPO — after Nasdaq rewrote its fast-entry rules for large IPOs. Index was weighted at 3× SpaceX's raw float (~1.3% weight), triggering $4B+ in forced passive buying. No existing company was removed; the Nasdaq-100 temporarily exceeds 100 constituents. Shares already down 28% from IPO high.
storylines.hidden_question: What does it mean when the rules of a major stock index get rewritten for a single company — and who actually benefits?

history.note: SpaceX confirmed in Nasdaq-100 after 15 trading days; rules rewritten to allow fast-track via market cap alone
$4B+ in passive ETF buying triggered; index temporarily exceeds 100 members; shares down 28% from IPO high

storylines.moment: Delivery Hero confirms Uber takeover talks — advanced negotiations on ~€10B deal
storylines.why_now: Delivery Hero officially confirmed today it is in advanced negotiations with Uber for a possible acquisition. Uber already owns 19.5% of Delivery Hero's capital plus 5.6% in options. The deal would make Uber the dominant food delivery platform across Europe, Asia, and Latin America — and set up a direct global battle with DoorDash.
storylines.hidden_question: What happens to food delivery gig work when one company owns everywhere?

history.note: Bloomberg reports Uber in advanced talks to acquire Delivery Hero at ~€10B
Delivery Hero officially confirms negotiations; Uber already holds 19.5% stake plus 5.6% in options

storylines.moment: Mistral releases Leanstral 1.5 — AI that mathematically proves code is correct
storylines.why_now: Leanstral 1.5 uses the Lean 4 proof assistant to provide formal mathematical verification that software behaves as intended — moving beyond code generation into correctness guarantees. Relevant for aviation, medical devices, and financial systems where bugs are unacceptable.
storylines.hidden_question: Why have computer scientists been able to mathematically prove software is correct since the 1970s — and why does almost no production code use it?

storylines.moment: Spain beats France 2-0 on Bastille Day — first final since 2010; England vs Argentina tomorrow
storylines.why_now: Spain defeated France 2-0 today at AT&T Stadium in Dallas — Oyarzabal penalty (22'), Porro (58') — ending France's tournament and Mbappé's best shot at a title. Spain has allowed just one goal in seven matches and is in their first World Cup final since 2010. Tomorrow: England vs Argentina in Atlanta, 3pm ET. Messi has 8 goals and 21 chances created — the most of any player at this tournament. England seeking their first final since 1966.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does every England vs. Argentina match feel less like a football game and more like unfinished business from a war?

history.note: France 2-0 Morocco (Mbappé, Dembélé); Spain 2-1 Belgium (Merino 88')
England beat Norway 2-1 AET; Argentina beat Switzerland 1-0 (Mac Allister 10')
Semis set: France vs Spain today (Bastille Day, Dallas); England vs Argentina tomorrow (Atlanta)
Argentine players said they'd win 'for the Malvinas'; Messi has 21 chances created, most of any player
Spain 2-0 France — Oyarzabal pen 22', Porro 58'. Spain in first final since 2010; allowed 1 goal in 7 matches. England vs Argentina tomorrow in Atlanta.

storylines.moment: Argentina in semifinal tomorrow — Egypt's FIFA protest still unresolved as Argentina chase the title
storylines.why_now: Argentina beat Switzerland to reach the semifinals. Egypt's formal protest over refereeing decisions in Argentina's improbable 3-2 comeback win remains open — FIFA has issued no ruling. Argentina plays England tomorrow in Atlanta. If they win the final, the protest will be resolved — or quietly dropped — with Argentina as world champions.
storylines.hidden_question: Has FIFA ever actually overturned a match result because of a refereeing protest — and if not, why do teams keep filing them?

history.note: Argentina completes stoppage-time comeback, wins 3-2
Egyptian FA officially protests refereeing decisions with FIFA
FIFA has not ruled on protest; Argentina faces Switzerland in QF
Argentina advance to semis; Egypt's protest still unresolved
Argentina plays England tomorrow in Atlanta; Egypt's protest still open — FIFA will likely let it expire quietly

storylines.moment: Live-action Moana opens — Dwayne Johnson reprises Maui a decade later
storylines.why_now: Disney's live-action remake of Moana debuted this weekend, with Dwayne Johnson reprising the role of Maui he voiced in the 2016 animated original. The film is tracking against a summer box office that has been dominated by sequels and remakes.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does Disney keep remaking animated films in live-action when the originals were made specifically because live-action couldn't do them justice?

storylines.moment: Emmy nominations: The Pitt leads with 25; Hacks gets 24 — record for a comedy series
storylines.why_now: The 2026 Emmy nominations were announced this week. The Pitt — a medical drama filmed entirely in real time across a single 15-hour ER shift — leads all shows with 25 nominations. Hacks received 24 nominations, a record for a comedy series in a single year. The nominations signal a prestige TV landscape increasingly dominated by experimental formats.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does a medical show filmed in real time — where the crew couldn't cut if something went wrong — lead the Emmy nominations in the same year that AI started writing everything else?

storylines.moment: Sam Neill dies at 78 — 'sudden and unexpected,' had been cancer-free after blood cancer treatment
storylines.why_now: The Jurassic Park actor and star of dozens of films died this week. He had gone public about his blood cancer diagnosis in 2023 and written a memoir; he had been considered cancer-free. His death was described as sudden and unexpected.
storylines.hidden_question: Why do we only start paying attention to an actor's body of work after they die?

storylines.moment: Original Iron Man comic page sells for $3.875M — record for Marvel comic art
storylines.why_now: The piece of original comic book art that first introduced readers to the Marvel Comics hero Iron Man sold at auction for $3,875,000, a new record for Marvel comic art. The page is from Tales of Suspense #39, published 1963 — a year when it cost 12 cents at a newsstand.
storylines.hidden_question: Why does the original page of a comic book sell for millions when you can read the same images in a reprint for free?
